sticker.alt: Full sticker design: a long horizontal illustration featuring 22 coral reef creatures and features
sticker._note: referenceWidth/referenceHeight MUST match the actual pixel dimensions of full-sticker.png exactly, or every creature's position will be misaligned.

creatures.name: Forereef coral community
creatures.alt: A clipart style design showing a collection of brown corals and an eel
creatures.description: The outer reef is called the fore reef and serves as the primary physical barrier between the outer ocean and lagoon. The forereef on Moorea has experienced many disturbances including bleaching and cyclones. Cyclones can sometimes act as a slate-clearing event, making space for new corals to settle and grow. After the most recent bleaching of 2019, the majority of corals that recovered were from the same genera, Pocillopora, shown here. Another bleaching event more recently resulted in the widespread deaths of these corals, which are now sitting on the reef, hollowing out from within.
papers: Burgess, S. C. et al. (2021). Response diversity in corals: Hidden differences in bleaching mortality among cryptic Pocillopora species. Ecology, 102(6), e03324.
Scafidi, K. C. et al. (2026). Remnant hollowed out dead coral skeleton branches defer coral community recovery. Plos one, 21(3), e0339527.

creatures.name: Fringing reef coral community
creatures.alt: A cluster of light brown corals with sea urchins around them
creatures.description: The reef closest to the island is called the fringing reef. This ecosystem is first to absorb any pollutants that run off the land into the water and, in Moorea, is characterized by many corals from the genus Porites. Here you can see the Porites rus corals creating a habitat for sea urchins, which can be found in high abundance in these nearshore environments.
papers: Edmunds, P. J. et al. (2025). Long‐term community dynamics are heterogeneous between fringing‐and fore‐reef habitats on an Indo‐Pacific coral reef. Ecosphere, 16(10), e70398.
Adjeroud, M. (1997). Factors influencing spatial patterns on coral reefs around Moorea, French Polynesia. Marine Ecology Progress Series, 159, 105-119

creatures.name: Poti Marara
creatures.alt: A red fishing boat, moving quickly over the water.
creatures.description: "Poti" means boat in Tahitian, and "marara" means flying fish, illustrating the speed of these vessels as their V shaped hull cuts seamlessly into the water. With a bow-positioned tiller, the driver is able to steer the boat with one hand, and spear fish from their high vantage point with the other. The front tiller steering allows for tight turns and easy mobility.
papers: Watch video

Blanchet, G. et al. (1988). The "poti marara" or the success of a small fishing boat fitted to needs. In Proceedings of the World Symposium on Fishing Gear and Fishing Vessel Design (pp. 244–246). Marine Institute. PDF Here

creatures.name: Blacktip reef shark
creatures.alt: A clipart style design showing a light gray reef shark.
creatures.description: The blacktip reef shark is easily identified by the black tipped marking on their dorsal fin. These charismatic reef-dwellers can be seen swimming around in turbid water in the shallow lagoons, or on the outer reef, curious about nearby divers. On Moorea, if you happen to see a blacktip on the outer reef, it is more likely a male, as females tend to prefer the lagoonal reef habitat.
papers: Mourier, J. et al. (2013). Population structure, spatial distribution and life‐history traits of blacktip reef sharks Carcharhinus melanopterus. Journal of fish biology, 82(3), 979-993.

Vignaud, T. et al. (2014). Blacktip reef sharks, C archarhinus melanopterus, have high genetic structure and varying demographic histories in their Indo‐Pacific range. Molecular Ecology, 23(21), 5193-5207

creatures.name: Magnificent sea anemone
creatures.alt: A clip art style design of a sea anemone in pink and yellow
creatures.description: The magnificent sea anemone is a charismatic creature on the reef, easily recognizable thanks to the mutually beneficial relationship between these anemone and the fishes that live on and around them. Since sea anemones are in the Phylum Cnidaria (the same one as sea jellies), they have similar stinging cells, called cnidocytes, located in their tentacles. So be careful not to touch these mesmerizing creatures, in case your skin reacts to the sting.
papers: Holbrook, S. J., & Schmitt, R. J. (2005). Growth, reproduction and survival of a tropical sea anemone (Actiniaria): benefits of hosting anemonefish. Coral reefs, 24(1), 67-73.

Titus, B. M., Bennett-Smith, M. F., Chiodo, T., & Rodriguez, E. (2024). The clownfish-hosting sea anemones (Anthozoa: Actiniaria): updated nomenclature, biogeography, and practical field guide. Zootaxa, 5506(1).

creatures.name: Polynesian anemonefish
creatures.alt: A clip art style design of a anemone fish
creatures.description: Anemonefish have an iconic muralist relationship with their hosts. The fishes gain physical protection for themselves and their clutches of eggs, while the fishes protect their hosts from other fish predators.
papers: O’Donnell, J. L. et al. (2025). A new species of Anemonefish from French Polynesia, Amphiprionmaohiensis,(Pomacentridae, Amphiprioninae), the Polynesian anemonefish. ZooKeys, 1244, 225.
Schligler, J. et al. (2022). Aggression of an orange-fin anemonefish to a blacktip reef shark: a potential example of fish mobbing? Marine Biodiversity 52:17.

creatures.name: Fourstripe Damselfish
creatures.alt: A clip art style design of a anemone fish
creatures.description: In addition to the easily recognizable Polynesian anemonefish, the four stripe damselfish can be found around reef habitats such as anemones and branching or corymbose corals. These organisms serve as protection for the damselfish from predators and until they outgrow this protection, these distinct fishes will stay within the protection of their hosts.
papers: Schmitt, R. J., & Holbrook, S. J. (1997, November). Temporal patterns of settlement of three species of damselfish of the genus Dascyllus (Pomacentridae) in the coral reefs of French Polynesia. In Proceedings of the 5th Indo-Pacific Fish Conference, Noumea (pp. 537-551).
McCafferty, S. Et al. (2002). Historical biogeography and molecular systematics of the Indo‐Pacific genus Dascyllus (Teleostei: Pomacentridae). Molecular Ecology, 11(8), 1377-1392

creatures.name: Coral Bommie with Macroalgae
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a yellow and green coral
creatures.description: In the shallow lagoons of Moorea, corals form large structures called "bommies", which are constructed of living and dead coral skeletons and can be multiple meters in diameter. These structures form ideal habitat for a diverse community of marine organisms, especially other invertebrates. Bommies can also be colonized by macroalgae, such as Turbinaria ornata as is shown here. This particular species of macroalgae is extremely hardy and is not eaten by many fishes, especially once it grows to full size, making it a strong competitor with corals for empty space on the reef.
papers: Schmitt, R. J. et al.(2022). Evaluating the precariousness of coral recovery when coral and macroalgae are alternative basins of attraction. Limnology and Oceanography, 67, S285-S297.
Bittick, S. J., Bilotti, N. D., Peterson, H. A., & Stewart, H. L. (2010). Turbinaria ornata as an herbivory refuge for associate algae. Marine Biology, 157(2), 317-323.

Cheh, A. S. et al. (2024). Algal turf accrues both costs and benefits to growth via association with adjacent stands of Turbinaria ornata, a rapidly expanding macroalga in the South Pacific. Marine Biology, 171(10), 200

creatures.name: Pacific double-saddle butterflyfish
creatures.alt: clipart style design of a striped reef fish
creatures.description: Butterflyfish are usually colorful and easy to spot amidst the patterns of a coral reef. Their distinct patterns serve as camouflage on the reef, with features like fake eye spots on their tails to confuse predators. On Moorea, their populations notably shifted after the Crown of Thorns Sea Star outbreak, as their densities are usually closely linked to that of the corals themselves.
papers: Berumen, M. L., & Pratchett, M. S. (2006). Recovery without resilience: persistent disturbance and long-term shifts in the structure of fish and coral communities at Tiahura Reef, Moorea. Coral reefs, 25(4), 647-653.

Bertucci, F. et al (2016). Natural cleaning of the black-lip pearl oyster Pinctada margaritifera by butterflyfishes (Chaetodon) in French Polynesia. Estuarine, Coastal and Shelf Science, 182, 270-273.

creatures.name: Parrotfish
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a colorful reef fish, shown from the side
creatures.description: Parrotfish are noticeable reef fishes, given their distinct coloring and relatively large size. These fish graze on the coral themselves, taking audible bites of the reef as they move around, excreting waste that contributes to the white sand on the benthos. Parrotfish are names for their distinct beak-looking mouth structures which allow them to more easily graze on the hard coral skeleton to get a bit including nutritious tissue, as well as their colorful scales, reminiscent of the colorful plumage of birds.
papers: Peyrot-Clausade, M. Et al. (2000). Sea urchin and fish bioerosion on La Reunion and Moorea reefs. Bulletin of Marine Science, 66(2), 477-485

Chazottes, V. Et al. (1995). Bioerosion rates on coral reefs: interactions between macroborers, microborers and grazers (Moorea, French Polynesia). Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology, 113(2-4), 189-198.

creatures.name: Octopus
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a red octopus emerging from a coral rock
creatures.description: Octopus are a favorite organism of the coral reefs for their charismatic behavior and curiosity. These incredible creatures can often be seen on the lagoon habitats in Moorea, typically sheltering within the coral heads scattered throughout the shallow reef.
papers: Scheel, D. Et al. (2017). Diversity in the diet of the predator Octopus cyanea in the coral reef system of Moorea, French Polynesia. Journal of Natural History, 51(43-44), 2615-2633.

creatures.name: Pincushion sea star
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a yellow and black spotted sea star
creatures.description: Culcita novaeguineae, or pincushion sea stars, are often found in the lagoons of Moorea, French Polynesia. They have a characteristically inflated appearance, making them look like brightly colored orbs, and they have an armored outer layer covered in calcareous ossicles, or "tiny bones". They are also corallivorous, meaning they eat corals, which they hunt for primarily at night. Pincushion sea stars have symbiotic relationships with several marine organisms, including commensal shrimp and star pearlfish, which are commonly found hiding under or even inside the sea star’s body cavity. [written by Annabelle Conti]
papers: Bergsma, G. S. (2011). Epibiotic mutualists alter coral susceptibility and response to biotic disturbance through cascading trait-mediated indirect interactions. Coral Reefs, 31(2), 461–469
McKeon, C. S., Stier, A. C., McIlroy, S. E., & Bolker, B. M. (2012). Multiple defender effects: synergistic coral defense by mutualist crustaceans. Oecologia, 169(4), 1095–1103.
V . Hawkins , S. (2006). Feeding Preference of the Cushion Star, Culcita novaeguineae in Mo'orea. Environmental Science Policy and Management, University of California, Berkeley, California.

creatures.name: Humpback whale
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a humpback whale breaching
creatures.description: Whale season is from roughly the middle of June until November in Moorea. During this time, you can hear the whales communicating underwater while diving, and may be lucky enough to see them breaching (jumping above the water) offshore throughout the day. Occasionally, the mothers will bring their babies near the reef, protecting them from predators by using the structural boundary of the reef as a natural barrier on one side.
papers: Raick, X. et al. (2021). From the reef to the ocean: revealing the acoustic range of the biophony of a coral reef (Moorea Island, French Polynesia). Journal of Marine Science and Engineering, 9(4), 420.

creatures.name: Pacific Bluefin Tuna
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a tuna, shown from the side
creatures.description: Pacific bluefin tuna are highly adapted ocean predators. With a streamlined fusiform (torpedo-shape) bodies, they can move through the water at incredible speeds. While offshore fishing was suspected to decrease immediately following colonization, today tuna fishing is a highly active industry and livelihood for many people living in French Polynesia. Industrial tuna fishing has been increasing more rapidly than previous decades since the 1980s, leading to a rise in over harvesting risk of these creatures, which take about 5 years to reach maturity.
papers: Barclay, K. (2014). History of industrial tuna fishing in the Pacific Islands. In Historical perspectives of fisheries exploitation in the Indo-Pacific (pp. 153-171). Dordrecht: Springer Netherlands.
ANGOT, M. (1959). Tuna fishing investigations in the South Pacific. Bull. South Pac. Comm, 9.

creatures.name: Pink whipray
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a stingray
creatures.description: These rays can be found in the shallow lagoon and sandy areas near coral reefs, where they hunt on the seafloor for invertebrates below the sand. Local stingrays have long been a tourist attraction on Moorea, with boats arriving to a set feeding site on the North West lagoon and attracting rays and reef sharks with food. Given the regular feeding patterns, this has driven behavior of rays on the reefs in Moorea, allowing researchers to reliably find and tag these individuals to study, comparing rays who receive food from tour boats, and those which occupy the reef without interference.
papers: D'Alberto, B. M. (2022). Conservation biology of wedgefishes (Family Rhinidae) and giant guitarfishes (Family Glaucostegidae) (Doctoral dissertation, James Cook University)

McCoy, B. (2008). Varying impact of human feeding on Pink Whiprays, Himantura fai, at two sites on Mo'orea. Printing from the Biology and Geomorphology of Tropical Islands course, University of California, Berkeley

creatures.name: Spearfisher
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing of a spearfished with red swim shorts
creatures.description: Spearfishing is an important part of subsidence fishing in areas with coral reefs, such as Moorea. Spearfishing is an art form, a favorite hobby, and an important livelihood that, with reefs changing globally, has been threatened in the face of changing coral reef ecosystems. In Moorea, a combination of permanent and rotating closures based on the ancient Polynesian practice of rāhui has protected fish communities on these reefs. Although the reefs in Moorea have changed in recent years, community members in Moorea do not report altering their fishing habits.
papers: Fabre, P. Et al. (2021). Contemporary Rāhui: placing Indigenous, conservation, and sustainability sciences in community-led conservation. Pacific Conservation Biology, 27(4), 451-463
Rassweiler, A. Et al. (2020). Perceptions and responses of Pacific Island fishers to changing coral reefs. Ambio, 49(1), 130-143
Strother, M. A. (2017). The Ecological and Political Dimensions of Local Knowledge Among Small-Scale Fishers in Moorea, French Polynesia. San Diego State University.

creatures.name: Submarine Groundwater Discharge
creatures.description: Submarine groundwater discharge (SGD) is a source of freshwater that can sometimes be found "bubbling" out from underground seeps onto the nearshore, fringing reef. SGD has cooler temperature, lower salinity and pH, and higher nutrient concentrations compared to seawater, so if you are ever out snorkeling and see a blurriness in the water, it could be the fresh groundwater mixing with the seawater. [written by Hannah Merges]
papers: Hagedorn, B. Et al. 2020. Evidence of freshened groundwater below a tropical fringing reef. Hydrogeology journal 28. Springer Science and Business Media LLC: 2501–2517.

Knee, K.L. et al. 2016. Assessment of Submarine Groundwater Discharge (SGD) as a Source of Dissolved Radium and Nutrients to Moorea (French Polynesia) Coastal Waters. Estuaries and Coasts. 39:1651 –1668. DOI 10.1007/s12237-016-0108-y.
Moore, W. S. 2010. The effect of submarine groundwater discharge on the ocean. Annual review of marine science 2. annualreviews.org: 59–88.
StoryMap

creatures.name: Manta Ray
creatures.alt: clipart style drawing showing two manta rays swimming
creatures.description: Manta rays are filter feeders, meaning they funnel large amounts of water though their mouths, catching things like zooplankton which accumulate near the surface of the water or bright lights at night, where zooplankton accumulate. Megafauna like Manta Rays are a signal of a healthy ecosystem and provide a unique opportunity for citizen science in places like Moorea, which receive a lot of tourism. Large and relatively easy to identify, people can record sightings of these beautiful creatures and report them to the Polynesian Shark Observatory (ORP) for use in papers such as those linked below.
papers: Séguigne, C. Et al. (2023). Citizen science provides valuable data to evaluate elasmobranch diversity and trends throughout the French Polynesia’s shark sanctuary. PLoS One, 18(3), e0282837.
